learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate understanding of international human rights law and the relevant institutional framework.
Identify and understand contemporary issues related to human rights, critically analyse challenges to the realisation of rights, and discuss concepts and ideas logically and effectively.
Present and communicate knowledge, understanding and critical awareness of issues concerning human rights.
Develop independent arguments and approaches for the protection of human rights.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment consists of multiple choice and short answer questions designed to enable students to demonstrate their understanding of the material covered in the first four seminars of the course. Students will have 80 minutes to complete the exam.

The quiz will be held in class in Seminar 5 on 23 September 2026. Students should make sure they arrive to class at or before 11.00am.

Any paper materials (notes, books, translation dictionaries etc) may be used. Electronic devices of any kind (including phones, computers, tablets) may not be used, including translation devices.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Draft Complaint to a Treaty Body
assessments.task_description:
The second assessment item will require students to show a developed understanding and critical awareness of international human rights law, as informed by the content of the seminars and further independent study.

This assessment involves preparation of a draft complaint to a treaty body – the United Nations Human Rights Committee –based on a hypothetical set of facts. The question will be made available on Learn.UQ four weeks prior to the due date. The word limit for the paper is 3,500 words. Citations in footnotes do not count to this limit, though any substantive text will. Words beyond the 3,500 word limit will not be marked.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 5 To Week 12(24 Aug - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

The seminar topics are available from the course blackboard site. There are eight seminars in the course. The seminars start in week 5.

Seminar topics:

  1. The Development and Sources of International Human Rights Law
  2. State Responsibility and Jurisdiction
  3. States Obligations: The Duty to Respect and Acceptable Limitations
  4. States Obligations: The Duties to Protect and Fulfil
  5. The Norm of Non-discrimination
  6. Mechanisms of Protection and Enforcement
  7. Critiques of Human Rights
  8. Emerging Challenges

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
